source_metadata.title_raw: Emel G. Wadhwani - A-19-029 - May 17, 2019 - Sacramento

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 17, 2019

Emel G. Wadhwani
Assistant Chief Counsel
Office of Chief Counsel
1001 I Street, 22nd Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 19-029

Dear Ms. Wadhwani:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Jean-Pierre Wolff, Ph.D., Chair of the Central Coast Region, Regional Water Quality Control Board, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the development and approval of the Central Coast Water Board's next regulatory program for discharges from irrigated agricultural lands ("New Order") have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial impact on Dr. Wolff's financial interests and prohibit his participation under the Act?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-029
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

No. Dr. Wolff does not have a prohibited financial interest in the New Order decisions.
While his business and real property interests in Wolff Vineyards, LLC are subject to increased fees
under the New Order, the increase is anticipated to be nominal, and therefore not material, under the
Act.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Jean-Pierre Wolff, Ph.D., is chair of the Regional Water Quality Control Board for the
Central Coast Region ("Board"). The Board regulates the discharge of waste from irrigated
agricultural operations. Several decades ago, the Regional Board implemented a conditional waiver
program, for groups and individuals, to comply with irrigation and storm water runoff discharges
which may affect water quality.² In 2012 the Board adopted Resolution R3-2012-0011, the
Conditional Waiver of Discharge Requirements for Discharges from Irrigated Lands ("2012
Order"), effective for a five-year cycle.³ In 2017, the Board adopted R3-2017-0002, ("2017
Order").⁴

The purpose of the past discharge Orders was to ensure that dischargers are not polluting
surface water or groundwater. The past Orders required dischargers to pay an annual fee, implement
management practices and conduct monitoring and reporting of irrigation and storm water runoff,
and, if failing to do so, be subject to progressive enforcement and possible monetary liability. The
Orders do not name specific growers and landowners of irrigated agricultural lands ("dischargers"),
and require all dischargers to enroll or seek individual waste discharge requirements.

Dr. Wolff's financial interests have not changed since the previous letters were issued and
are as follows:

Business Entity: Dr. Wolff owns 49% of Wolff Vineyards LLC ("Wolf LLC") and
the remainder is held by his wife and children. Dr. Wolff has an investment of
greater than $2,000 in, is employed by, Wolff LLC.

Real Property: Wolff LLC has owned and operated a certified sustainable vineyard
in San Luis Obispo for the past twenty years ("Wolff Vineyards"). The vineyard
covers 125 acres. Of those acres, 100 acres are currently irrigated, necessitating
enrollment in the current 2017 Order.

² The conditional waiver program provides a means for agricultural dischargers to comply with federal and
state water quality laws, and the Regional Water Quality Control Board's plans and policies. See,
https://alfalfa.ucdavis.edu/†symposium/proceedings/2003/03-19.pdf. Viewed April 23, 2019.

³ The Commission issued two advice letters to Dr. Wolff regarding the 2012 Order and its implementation.
(Jahr Advice Letter, No. A-13-045a; and Jahr Advice Letter, No. A-14-031.)

⁴ See Jahr Advice Letter, No. A-16-146. Reflecting changes in the applicable regulations, we advised that Dr.
Wolff was not prohibited from participating in the 2017 Order, based on facts indicating the decisions would have only
a "nominal, inconsequential and insignificant" financial effect on Wolf LLC.

File No. A-19-029
Page No. 3

Source of Income: Wolf LLC, and its clients, are sources of income from which
Dr. Wolff has received $500 or more within the past 12 months.

Under the 2017 Order, Wolff Vineyards is a Tier 1 discharger and subject to the least
stringent of its requirements. Tier 1 includes those dischargers that present the lowest risk to water
quality. Wolff Vineyards' Tier 1 status is based on its Sustainability in Practice ("SIP") Certified
status. Wolff Vineyards voluntarily participates in the SIP certification program for farmers and
vintners, which has more stringent requirements than the 2012 or 2017 Orders.5 Dr. Wolff's
annual 2018 costs to participate in the 2017 Order include a third-party annual monitoring fee of
$434 for surface water quality monitoring and an annual SIP certification fees of $1775, for a total
of $2,209. The 2017 Order expires on March 8, 2020.

The Board has initiated proceedings to adopt a new regulatory program ("New Order"), to
replace the 2017 Order. The Board will hold several workshops or hearings to give direction to staff
on developing a proposed program. In March, staff and stakeholders presented revised proposals,
but no direction was given to staff at the workshop. Dr. Wolff limited his participation, while
awaiting this response.6

The New Order may increase fees as described below. Under the highest projections, Wolff
LLC's fees are expected to increase by $87 for increased surface water monitoring, and $180 for
SIP certification, for a total increase of $267. This will result in an estimated total of $2,476 in fees
for the LLC to participate in the New Order and the SIP Certification Program.

The following bullets summarize the important required and discretionary items currently
under consideration by staff:

1. Water Quality Objectives and Time for Compliance.

The Board must incorporate provisions established by law that require compliance with
water quality objectives, the Board's discretion is limited to requiring compliance
immediately or on a time schedule, with set milestones for progress towards water quality
objectives, consistent with the holding in Monterey Coastkeeper v. State Water Resources
Control Board (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 342.

You provided additional information by phone that Wolff LLC is most likely already in
compliance with these objectives due to its current practices. Therefore, you do not
anticipate that the discretion on time for compliance will have any impact on the LLC.

2. Increased Nitrogen Management and Reporting.

_____________
5 SIP Certification requires that no high-risk pesticides or ground contaminates are used. It also includes
regular inspections, as well as outreach and education, by farm advisors to ensure growers are meeting environmental
protection standards.

6 Updated and additional facts were provided by email on April, 4, 2019.

File No. A-19-029
Page No. 4

The Board must incorporate increased nitrogen management and reporting requirements: preparation and certification an irrigation and nitrogen management plan, and reporting data; sampling farm drinking wells for nitrates, and training/outreach established by the State Water Resources Control Board's WQ 2018-0002 ESJ Precedential Order. The Board has no discretion on incorporating these requirements.

Wolf LLC already incurs the costs associated with the precedential requirements through its participation in the SIP certification program, so no increased costs are anticipated for the LLC.

3. General Waste Discharge Requirements ("WDRs").

Staff may recommend that the Board issue the New Order as WDRs instead of issuing another conditional waiver. Converting the order to general WDRs in and of itself does not require substantive changes to the order requirements. Enrollees in orders regulating discharges from irrigated agricultural lands must pay annual fees. By regulation, the State Water Board, and not the Board, sets the amount of the fees.⁷ The status of a general order as a conditional waiver, versus waste discharge requirements, will not affect the enrollment fees for the LLC.

4. Edge-of-Field Monitoring.

Staff may recommend edge-of-field monitoring for discharges to surface water for certain growers or categories of growers.

The 2017 Order currently does not require edge-of-field discharge monitoring for Tier 1 dischargers; and, Wolff Vineyards does not have discharges to surface water. Because Wolff Vineyards does not discharge to surface water, it is expected that the LLC will continue to satisfy any surface water monitoring requirements through participation in the third-party receiving water monitoring program, regardless of any changes to the monitoring requirements in the New Order.

5. Limitations on Surface Water Discharges.

Staff may recommend numeric limitations on surface water discharges for nutrients, pesticides, and sediment. Dischargers exceeding limitations may be required to implement additional management practices or may be subject to penalties.

Because Wolff Vineyards does not have discharges to surface water, any such recommended provisions are not projected to increase the LLC's costs of compliance.

___________________________

⁷ (See, Cal. Code Regs., tit. 23, §2200.6,)

File No. A-19-029
Page No. 5

6. Buffer Zone from the Surface Water Bodies.

Staff may recommend that all growers adjacent to surface water bodies establish a buffer
zone from the surface water bodies where no farming operations will take place and where
native vegetative cover will be maintained. Tier 1 dischargers are not required to maintain
such setbacks under the 2017 Order. They may incur costs under the New Order for
establishing the buffer zones and may have costs associated with loss of cropland in the
buffer zones.

Wolff LLC currently implements riparian setbacks in accordance with the most recent
recommendations of the Natural Resource Conservation Service of the US Department of
Agriculture, the Coastal San Luis Resource Conservation District, the California
Department of Fish and Wildlife, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration,
and the California Salmonid Restoration Federation. It is unclear at this point in the
proceedings whether the New Order will have buffer and setback requirements that will
exceed the recommendations of these entities, but it is anticipated that the LLC will not
incur additional costs because of any recommended new requirements.

7. Increased Monitoring: Surface Receiving Water

A coalition of environmental groups recommends expanded monitoring, to be performed
either individually, by growers, or by a third-party surface receiving water monitoring
program, as well as follow-up monitoring for exceedances.

Staff identified this item could increase costs to dischargers. Following the March
workshop, staff now estimates that third-party surface receiving water monitoring program
fees are likely to increase only for dischargers in watersheds where a program of significant
follow-up monitoring is developed. Wolff Vineyards is not in this type of a watershed and
therefore unlikely to see increases in its fees. Even if the follow-up monitoring costs are
distributed evenly throughout the third-party monitoring areas, so that dischargers in
watersheds with no follow-up monitoring bear some of the cost of the increased monitoring,
staff anticipates that the fees will increase by 10-20%. Wolff Vineyards currently
contributes $434 annually to the third-party surface receiving water monitoring
program. Under the highest projections for increased costs, Wolff Vineyards' fees would
increase by $87 under the New Order.

The overwhelming majority of dischargers enrolled under the 2017 Order are enrolled in the
third-party surface receiving water monitoring program. Specifically, of the 4489 ranches
enrolled under the existing order, 4454 are enrolled in the program. Similar enrollment is
expected under the New Order. As stated above, depending on how the program is
structured under the 2017 Order, the fee increases could be proportionally higher for the
class of dischargers operating in watersheds where a program of follow-up monitoring is
developed. (Again, Wolff Vineyards is not in a one of these watersheds.) However, if the
follow-up monitoring costs are distributed evenly throughout the third-party monitoring
areas, the fee increases are expected to be applied proportionally or by the same percentage
to all the ranches enrolled in the third-party surface receiving water monitoring program.

File No. A-19-029
Page No. 6

8. SIP Certification Costs

Staff may recommend that certification programs, such as SIP, adopt changes related to
documentation, auditing, and certification requirements. Staff estimates that any proposed
changes could result in SIP certification fee increases of 15 percent or lower.

For Wolf LLC, a 15 percent increase would represent an annual increase of $180
in certification fees. As noted previously, SIP certification is voluntary and
enrollment in the certification program will not be required by the New Order.

The SIP certification program is specific to vineyards, with additional
certification programs for other crops in development for the New Order. 147
vineyards, constituting roughly 30 percent of the acreage of vineyards under the
2017 Order, are enrolled in the SIP certification program. The certification fee
increases are expected to be applied proportionally or by the same percentage to
all vineyards enrolled in the SIP program and further would be reflected in the
fees of any ranches that will be enrolled in certification programs currently in
development.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interests relevant
to Dr. Wolff are:

• Business Entity: Wolff Vineyards LLC (Section 87103(a) and (d).)

• Real Property: the LLC's 125-acre vineyard, which includes 55 acres of irrigated land
enrolled in the 2012 Order and 2017 Order under Tier 1. (Section 87103(b).)

• Source of Income: the LLC is a source of income; as are any clients who have provided or
promised $500 or more in the 12 months prior to a decision. (Section 87103(c).)

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the official's financial interest
is explicitly involved as a subject of the decision, which includes instances where a decision would
modify any fees that apply to the parcel.8 (Regulation 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(3).9) Where the

8 A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, or if the
decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) - (6). (Regulation 18701.)

9 Regulation 18702.2 was amended by the Commission on January 17, 2019 effective March, 2019.

File No. A-19-029
Page No. 7

financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, the foreseeability standard is whether the
financial effect can be recognized as a "realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).)

Under the facts provided, is a realistic possibility that the New Order will impose increased
fees applicable to the LLC's agricultural property, and the financial effect is reasonably foreseeable.

Business Entity and Source of Income

Regulation 18702.1(b) sets forth the applicable materiality standard for a business interest
under a general regulation.10 Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that the financial effect is material if a
prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's
financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity.

The New Order is expected to increase the LLC's fees by $267, with no other impact on the
business. Based on the minimal impact on the business, it is not likely this would contribute to a
change in the business's perceived value, and the standard for materiality is not met.

Real Property

Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(3), the financial effect of a government decisions is
reasonably foreseeable and material where the decision would "modify any taxes, fees, or
assessments that apply to the parcel." As noted above, the New Order decisions will modify fees
applicable the LLC's irrigated agricultural property. However, Regulation 18702(b) provides that
notwithstanding a determination of materiality, the financial effect of a government decision is
deemed not material, inconsequential, or insignificant. The total fees involved in the
New Order amount to $2,746, for an increase of less than $300 in annual fees from the fees
associated with the previous order. As we advised in regards to the 2017 Order, the New Order will
result in a "nominal, inconsequential and insignificant" financial effect under these circumstances.

Public Generally Exception

Further, we note the public generally exception, Regulation 18703(e)(1) is not applicable to
these particular facts. Added in 2015, Regulation 18703(e), "Specific Rules for Special
Circumstances" clarifies that the public generally exception permits officials to participate in decisions
regarding general fees that apply to the entire jurisdiction if the official establishes:

The decision establishes or adjusts assessments, taxes, fees, or rates for water,
sewer, or other broadly provided public services or facilities that are applied
equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to the official's interest and
other businesses, properties, or individuals subject to the assessment, tax, fee, or
rate.

10 Under Regulation 18702.3 the materiality standard for a source of income that is a business entity is the
same as for an interest in the business entity itself.

File No. A-19-029
Page No. 8

The Board's formation and implementation of the New Order arises from a broadly-provided public interest in ensuring overall public water quality, and the public task of addressing agricultural wastewater. However, the facts indicate that some fees may be higher for dischargers located in particular watersheds, and therefore may not be applied proportionally. Therefore, the Regulation 18703(e)(1) exception is not applicable to the New Order decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:aja

participation under the Act?

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of 
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interests relevant 
to Dr. Wolff are: 
• 
Business Entity: Wolff Vineyards LLC (Section 87103(a) and (d).) 
• 
Real Property: the LLC's 125-acre vineyard, which includes 55 acres of irrigated land 
enrolled in the 2012 Order and 2017 Order under Tier 1. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
Source of Income: the LLC is a source of income; as are any clients who have provided or 
promised $500 or more in the 12 months prior to a decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the official's financial interest 
is explicitly involved as a subject of, the decision, which includes instances where a decision would 
modify any fees that apply to the parcel.8 (Regulation 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(3). 9) Where the 
8 A financial interest is the subject ofa proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, or if the 
decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) - (6). (Regulation 18701.) 
9 Regulation 18702.2 was amendect°by the Commission on January 17, 2019 effective March, 2019.  
financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, the foreseeability standard is whether the 
financial effect can be recognized as a "realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Under the facts provided, is a realistic possibility that the New Order will impose increased 
fees applicable to the LLC's agricultural property, and the financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. 
Business Entity and Source of Income 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) sets forth the applicable materiality standard for a business interest 
under a general regulation. 10 Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that the financial effect is material if a 
prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's 
financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity. 
The New Order is expected to increase the LLC's fees by $267, with no other impact on the 
business. Based on the minimal impact on the business, it is not likely this ;would contribute to a 
change in the business's perceived value, and the standard for materiality is not met. 
Real Property 
Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(3), the financial effect of a government decisions is 
reasonably foreseeable and material where the decision would "modify any taxes, fees, or 
assessments that apply to the parcel." As noted above, the New Order decisions will modify fees 
applicable the LLC's irrigated agricultural property. However, Regulation 18702(b) provides that 
notwithstanding a determination of materiality, the financial effect of a government decision is 
deemed not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. The total fees involved in the 
New Order amount to $2,746, for an increase ofless than $300 in annual fees from the fees 
associated with the previous order. As we advised in regards to the 2017 Order, the New Order will 
result in a "nominal, inconsequential and insignificant" financial effect under these circumstances. 
Public Generally Exception 
Further, we note the public generally exception, Regulation 18703(e)(l) is not applicable to 
these particular facts. Added in 2015, Regulation 18703(e), "Specific Rules for Special 
Circumstances" clarifies that the public generally exception permits officials to participate in decisions 
regarding general fees that apply to the entire jurisdiction if the official establishes: 
The decision establishes or adjusts assessments, taxes, fees, or rates for water, 
utility, or other broadly provided public services or facilities that are applied 
equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to the official's interest and 
other businesses, properties, or individuals subject to the assessment, tax, fee, or 
rate. 
10 Under Regulation 18702.3 the materiality standard for a source of income that is a business entity is the 
same as for an interest in the business entity itself.  
The Board's formation and implementation of the New Order arises from a broadly-
provided public interest in ensuring overall public water quality, and the public task of addressing 
agricultural wastewater. ·However, the facts indicate that some fees may be higher for dischargers 
located in particular watersheds, and therefore may not be applied proportionally. Therefore, the 
Regulation 18703( e )(1) exception is not applicable to the New Order decisions. 
LKH:aja

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the development and approval of the.Central Coast Water Board's next regulatory 
program for discharges from irrigated agricultural lands ("New Order") have a reasonably 
foreseeable and material financial impact on Dr. Wolffs financial interests and prohibit his 
participation under the Act?

CONCLUSION: No. Dr. Wolff does not have a prohibited financial interest in the New Order decisions. 
While his business and real property interests in Wolff Vineyards, LLC are subject to increased fees 
under the New Order, the increase is anticipated to be nominal, and therefore not material, under the 
Act.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 May 17, 2019 Emel G. Wadhwani Assistant Chief Counsel Office of Chief Counsel 1001 I Street, 22nd Floor Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. 19-029 Dear Ms. Wadhwani: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Jean-Pierre Wolff, Ph.D., Chair of the Central Coast Region, Regional Water Quality Control Board, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION Does the development and approval of the Central Coast Water Board's next regulatory program for discharges from irrigated agricultural lands ("New Order") have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial impact on Dr. Wolff's financial interests and prohibit his participation under the Act? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-029 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION No. Dr. Wolff does not have a prohibited financial interest in the New Order decisions. While his business and real property interests in Wolff Vineyards, LLC are subject to increased fees under the New Order, the increase is anticipated to be nominal, and therefore not material, under the Act. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER Jean-Pierre Wolff, Ph.D., is chair of the Regional Water Quality Control Board for the Central Coast Region ("Board"). The Board regulates the discharge of waste from irrigated agricultural operations. Several decades ago, the Regional Board implemented a conditional waiver program, for groups and individuals, to comply with irrigation and storm water runoff discharges which may affect water quality.² In 2012 the Board adopted Resolution R3-2012-0011, the Conditional Waiver of Discharge Requirements for Discharges from Irrigated Lands ("2012 Order"), effective for a five-year cycle.³ In 2017, the Board adopted R3-2017-0002, ("2017 Order").⁴ The purpose of the past discharge Orders was to ensure that dischargers are not polluting surface water or groundwater. The past Orders required dischargers to pay an annual fee, implement management practices and conduct monitoring and reporting of irrigation and storm water runoff, and, if failing to do so, be subject to progressive
